questions.question: What two production failure modes do roots and elicitation respectively fix?
options: Hard-coded path assumptions (server touches the wrong directories) and ambiguous under-specified arguments mid-tool-call
questions.explanation: Roots scope the server to user-declared URIs, fixing the server that assumed ~/notes when the user's notes live elsewhere. Elicitation pauses a tool call to ask the user which of three matching "TPS report" notes to delete — instead of guessing catastrophically.

questions.question: Why are roots a CLIENT primitive rather than something the server declares?
options: Roots encode the user's consent — the user told the client which directories the server may touch, and the server cannot widen that scope
questions.explanation: The root list is the user's consent model, declared by the client at initialize. A spec-compliant server treats it as a hard boundary and rejects any file operation outside it — and updates when notifications/roots/list_changed arrives.

questions.question: What are the three possible outcomes of an elicitation request?
options: accept (user filled the form), decline (user closed it), cancel (user aborted the whole tool call)
questions.explanation: The client renders the requested form schema, and the response's action field distinguishes a filled form from a dismissed one from a full abort of the underlying tool call — three different things the server must handle.

questions.question: What schema limitation applies to elicitation forms in v1?
options: Schemas must be flat — nested objects are not supported, and SDKs typically reject anything deeper than one layer
questions.explanation: Elicitation form schemas are single-layer: properties like an enum-constrained note_id and a boolean confirm work; nested objects don't. Complex input belongs in URL-mode elicitation (SEP-1036) — which is experimental through H1 2026.

questions.question: Who enforces the root boundary at runtime?
options: The client blocks out-of-root file operations at the OS level
The server itself — roots are honored by spec-compliant server code; the client cannot intercept the server's file I/O
questions.explanation: Roots are a contract, not a sandbox: the server is code the user chose to trust, and spec-compliant servers reject out-of-root reads and writes themselves. Real OS-level sandboxing is a separate, complementary defense.
